title: Oil on water marbling
subtitle: Floating oil-based paint on plain water for loose, unpredictable patterns
excerpt: Oil on water marbling is the simplest marbling technique: oil-based paint or printing ink dropped onto plain water floats and spreads in swirling patterns that are captured on paper. The results are less controlled than carrageenan marbling and have a characteristically loose, unpredictable quality. No size is needed; water alone is the base.
sourceNotes: The oil-on-water technique appears in early European decorated paper manuals including Johann Christoph Weigel's Papier-Mahler (c.1720, Nuremberg). Documentation also in Charles Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853), chapter on simple water marbling. Both pre-1928.

glossaryTerms.definition: A marbling technique in which oil-based paint or printing ink is floated on plain water. The oil does not mix with the water and spreads across the surface, where it can be captured on unsized paper.
In oil-on-water marbling, neither the paper nor the water needs any pre-treatment with size or alum. The oil-based paint adheres to plain unsized paper on contact.

content.text: requires no size, no alum, and no special paint preparation. The plain water surface and the

process make it accessible to beginners, though the patterns are less precise and cannot be combed or manipulated in the way carrageenan patterns can. The paint spreads wherever it will, influenced by the currents in the water and the order of colour application. Paper is laid on and lifted immediately.

attrs.heading: Kit for oil on water marbling

items.name: Shallow tray or roasting tin, slightly larger than the paper
Cold water
items.qty: Enough to fill the tray to 3 to 4 cm depth

items.name: Oil-based printing ink or artist's oil paint thinned with white spirit to a flowing consistency
White spirit for thinning and clean-up
Eyedropper or small brush for dropping colour
Plain unsized paper (cartridge or copy paper)

attrs.heading: Ventilation and disposal
attrs.body: White spirit is flammable and produces fumes. Work in a ventilated space. Dispose of the used water at a hazardous waste collection point, not down the drain.

content.text: Thin the oil paint or printing ink with white spirit until it flows freely from a brush or eyedropper. The consistency should be similar to single cream: thin enough to drop but not so thin it disperses immediately to a faint film. Too thick and the paint sits in lumps; too thin and it spreads so far it loses colour density.
Floating the colour and lifting the print

Fill the tray with cold water and let it settle. Carefully drop the first colour onto the surface from about 5 cm above the water. It will spread into a large disk. Add the second and third colours in the same way, in different areas of the tray.

The colours interact as they spread and meet. To move them further, blow gently across the surface or stir lightly with a cocktail stick. Keep movements minimal: the natural flow of the colours produces the most coherent patterns.

Lower the paper onto the surface in a single smooth motion (centre first, edges down). Press gently with the backs of your fingers. Lift from one corner immediately. The oil paint transfers to the paper on contact.

Place the sheet paint-side up on a thick pad of newspaper to dry. Oil-based paint takes several hours to dry fully. Skim any remaining paint from the water surface with a strip of newspaper and repeat.

attrs.heading: Common problems with oil marbling
attrs.intro: Problems in paint spreading and paper adhesion.

items.symptom: Paint clumps on the water surface and does not spread
items.cause: Paint too thick, or water surface contaminated with dried paint from a previous session.
items.fix: Thin the paint further with white spirit. Replace the water and clean the tray if the surface is contaminated.

items.symptom: Print fades to a very pale ghost on the paper
items.cause: Paint spread too thin across too large an area.
items.fix: Use a smaller tray or drop more paint per colour to maintain colour density.
